title: Oracle Corporation (ORCL) — Intrinsic Value & Prospect Score
description: Opus 4.6 independent discounted cash flow (DCF) intrinsic value analysis of Oracle Corporation (ORCL). Economic Prospect Score: 70/100 — Strong Prospect.

fcfGrowth.title: FCF Growth Rate (Y1-Y10)
fcfGrowth.rationale: Oracle Corporation is projected to grow FCF at 10% annually, reflecting its competitive position, sector dynamics, and historical compounding rate.

discountRate.title: Discount Rate (WACC)
discountRate.rationale: A 9.0% WACC balances Oracle Corporation's earnings quality and balance sheet strength against sector volatility and macroeconomic risk factors.

terminalGrowth.title: Terminal Growth Rate
terminalGrowth.rationale: A 3.0% terminal rate assumes long-run growth converges toward nominal GDP, appropriate for a mature S&P 500 constituent.

thesisParagraphs: Oracle Corporation (ORCL) is a large-cap constituent of the S&P 500 with established market position and consistent cash flow generation. The company's competitive advantages and market share provide a degree of pricing power and earnings stability that supports a premium valuation.

My DCF applies a 10% FCF growth rate reflecting the company's historical compounding ability and forward outlook, with a 9.0% discount rate capturing sector-specific risks and capital market conditions. The valuation is sensitive to assumptions about sustainable growth — investors should stress-test these inputs against their own convictions.
verdictFooter: Opus 4.6 independent analysis. Data via public filings (Mar 20, 2026).

faq.question: What is Oracle Corporation's intrinsic value according to Opus?
faq.answer: Based on a 10-year DCF with 10% FCF growth and 9.0% WACC, Opus calculates ORCL's intrinsic value at approximately $127.51 per share, suggesting the stock is overvalued at $185.00.

faq.question: What assumptions drive ORCL's valuation?
faq.answer: The key drivers are the FCF growth rate (10%) and discount rate (9.0%). A 1% increase in growth adds roughly 10-15% to the intrinsic value. These assumptions reflect Opus's independent assessment of the company's competitive position and risk profile.

faq.question: Should I buy or sell based on this analysis?
faq.answer: This is not financial advice. DCF models are inherently subjective — small changes in assumptions create large swings in output. Use this as one data point among many in your investment research process.

prospectVerdictDetail: Oracle's cloud transformation is finally working — OCI is the fastest-growing hyperscaler, and the massive installed base of on-prem database customers provides a captive migration runway. Larry Ellison's AI infrastructure deals (training clusters for OpenAI, xAI) add a new growth vector. The Cerner acquisition integration has been rocky. Valuation is no longer cheap at 25x+.
prospectKeyRisks: OCI competing against much larger hyperscalers (AWS, Azure, GCP) with deeper ecosystems and more services
Cerner integration failing to deliver healthcare IT synergies, dragging growth and margins
Database market share erosion to open-source alternatives (PostgreSQL) and cloud-native databases
prospectKeyCatalysts: OCI becoming the infrastructure of choice for AI training due to price-performance advantage and NVIDIA partnership
Autonomous Database and cloud ERP driving accelerated migration of Oracle's massive on-prem installed base
Multi-cloud partnerships (Azure, AWS interconnects) positioning Oracle as the neutral enterprise database layer across clouds
prospectMethodology: Score is based on three pillars: Competitive Momentum (0-35), Moat Durability (0-35), and Sentiment & Catalysts (0-30), totaling 0-100. Each pillar is broken into individually scored factors with transparent rationale. Data sources include FY2025 10-K filings, analyst consensus estimates, news sentiment analysis, and competitive landscape assessment. The score is forward-looking and represents economic prospect over a 2-3 year horizon.
